We've learned that the technology hooks in the form of innovative products are key importance in securing uptake of our integrated offers. That's why the innovation pipeline that I showed you underpins our confidence in achieving further gain in the coming years. 
I'm handing you now back to John for some more detail on this and for an overview of our profitability objectives.

sections.speaker: John Ramsay
sections.content:
Thank you, Mike. Slide 36. I showed you a version of this chart in February. And today, I would like to explain how we are going to address the different levels of profitability in Seeds in order to achieve the gross margin of over 50% for the business in 2018. Both vegetables and the bulk of diverse field crops already operate above this level of profitability and growth momentum in both areas will have a positive impact. 
However, the greatest contribution to margin improvement will come from corn in both North America and Rest of the World and from soybean and cereals. The next 2 slides outline the drivers of this. 
Starting with corn on Slide 37. Profitability in North America is already high, thanks to our proprietary trait platform. As Mike should earlier, we plan to further leverage our traits through licensing. Our branded business will benefit from our focus on the Seed Advisor channel to drive an increased footprint. At the same time, we will realize the disruptive value of ENOGEN technology, which enables us to generate additional seed sales and also pull-through Crop Protection chemistry. 
Our traits will also play an important role in improving corn profitability in the Rest of the World. VIPTERA is the lepidoptera trait without resistance issues in Latin America and this is recognized by our large number of licensees. 
In Asia Pacific, we are leading the expansion of traits in Vietnam. And finally in order to improve our cost structure for corn seed, we will optimize production locations in Europe closer to end markets. 
Turning to soybean and cereals on Slide 38, firstly soybean. The current level of profitability is inadequate and we are determined to improve returns. In Latin America with our new business partner model, we have created an entirely new model, which will improve profitability. 
In the past, we would license our genetics to multipliers in exchange for royalty. A multiplier grows seed and then sells the seed under their own brand. We also operated a branded model, which managed the entire value chain and was more profitable, but was challenging to scale up. We have, therefore, now created our integrated business partner model. And under this model, our partners multiply our seeds and sell them on to their customers, but with the Syngenta brand allowing premium pricing. 
Our partners are also excited about this as it has enabled them better access to high-quality genetics, underpinning an increase in operations and profitability. We will also see a mix benefit from sales of INTACTA and from the ramp-up of our integrated high-yield soybean protocol. We will continue to invest in our cereals business, and at the same time achieve higher profitability. 
We continue to ramp up HYVIDO with the launch of new, improved hybrids at a lower cost of production. And our cost structure will also benefit from the outsourcing of hybrid supply. 
Now please turn to Slide 39 for an explanation of our Seeds portfolio management. Our Seeds business can be divided into 3 areas of strategic focus: driving profitability improvement, maximizing high-value businesses and long-term industry-leading investments. The portfolio of management tools we have available are applicable across all 3 areas. All businesses are screened for the potential for AOL efficiency savings. For example, our field crops business is currently implementing a common sales and operational planning end-to-end process to help enhance the customer experience. 
Seeds businesses demonstrating the potential for pull-though of chemical sales are prioritized to underpin our integrated strategy. A notable example would be our HYVIDO barley business as well as the ongoing investment into hybrid wheat. 
Where necessary, we will also make changes to the business model as we have done with Latin American soybean. If we are not able to achieve on a timely basis, the targeted level of profitability including these tools, we are prepared to explore JV and divestment options, particularly if those options enable us to increase the strategic value inherent in the crops sooner than we could otherwise do. 
Please turn to Slide 40. Seeds margin improvement is part of a company-wide Accelerating Operational Leverage program, which is well under way. And as you can see from the first half sales results, it's already yielding substantial benefits. And today, I would like to provide a little more insight into the flexibility we have under the program. At the launch of AOL in early 2014, the majority of the contribution was expected to be generated by leverage. As the program stands today, the EBITDA margin contribution of 5 percentage points is comprised of around 60% cost savings and 40% leverage. 
Looking ahead, depending on market conditions, we have within the program the flexibility to refine this ratio in order to continue to target the margin improvement. With the first half savings delivered, we're firmly on track to meet the full year target of $265 million in 2015. And as I look ahead to 2016, planned initiatives are already well advanced. 
Let me take you through our EBITDA margin bridge to 2018 on Slide 41. This slide sets out how we plan to achieve our 2018 margin target. Starting on the left with the 2014 actual EBITDA margin at 2015 exchange rates to provide a baseline. My assumption on currencies beyond 2015 is for any movement to be broadly neutral. 
The overall sales growth assumption over the period is around 4% compound average to 2018, substantially below the historic market growth average. On this basis, volume leverage will contribute 2 percentage points to margin. The price contribution to margin of 3 percentage points reflects an underlying assumption of around 1% price increase per year. 
I have already discussed the AOL contribution to margin of 5 percentage points. The 1 percentage point improvement in COGS is largely realized from the impact of lower oil prices on raw materials and this will materialize mainly in 2016. Beyond this time frame, my assumption is neutral on raw materials. 
Cost inflation will remain an ongoing feature of our business and is largely associated with a significant presence in emerging markets. The price increases I just mentioned cover inflation in full on a dollar-for-dollar basis. The negative contribution in other is comprised mainly of non-repeating 2014 items, the largest of which is the pension scheme benefit realized in the second half of 2014. 
The box on the top right shows you the key ratio evolution to 2018 and from this you can see the biggest contribution will come from gross profit. As I now stand back and look at our 2015 results, we are already seeing clear benefits from the uptick in innovation and the ramp-up of new product launches. And profitability is being improved through AOL as we have in the first half results seen the benefits start to come through. I also take confidence in the extent to which company resources across the group are already focused on profitability improvement. It's my belief we are now well on track for delivery of our margin target of 24% to 26% in 2018. 
And let me now hand you back to Mike.

sections.speaker: Michael Mack
sections.content:
Thanks, John. Let me conclude by summarizing now why we think the market underestimates Syngenta's prospects, both in terms of growth and profitability, on Slide 43. 
Syngenta is the undisputed world leader in a Crop Protection market that is growing faster than anyone would have believed 10 years ago. In Seeds, while we're #3, we are increasingly realizing value from our trait technology while using our Seeds presence to pull-through more chemistry. 
We have 4 years’ experience adapting integrated go-to-market models at a territory level and clear evidence that our strategy is working. It has enhanced our already leading emerging market presence and is now gaining traction in the developed markets as well. 
In a technology-driven industry, no other company can boast innovation potential like ours. With 2 blockbuster launches in the space of 2 years and $6 billion in peak sales potential for our new and pipeline products, we expect to gain share, while maintaining a high level of profitability in Crop Protection. 
At the same time, as John just said, we are taking determined actions to improve our profitability in seeds. As a result, we are exceptionally positioned to deliver leading innovation and high returns in an industry that has decades of growth potential ahead of it. 
That concludes our presentation today. And I would like to now open up the call for questions. Operator?

sections.speaker: Tony Jones
sections.content: Tony Jones, Redburn in London. I've got 3 questions. So firstly, on the EBITDA bridge, could you be specific about what the contribution was from the change in revenue recognition in South America? We've got the sales, but I wasn't sure what margins apply? And then sort of related to that, could you also highlight if there was any year-on-year gain in here that came from licensing income and whether that was just GreenLeaf or from some other factor? And then also on CapEx, the PPE CapEx was, I think, $190 million, so quite a bit lower year-on-year, lower than we were modeling. But I thought previous indications have been that we should expect an absolute increase this year and then another step-up next year. Is this just phasing or should we now model in much more prudent CapEx allocation?

sections.speaker: John Ramsay
sections.content: Thanks, Tony. Just on CapEx, we typically -- we get variations year-on-year, but I think this year is indicative of the size of the CapEx demand. And we work on the basis of around 4.5% of sales on a planning basis and I'd still see that as a good guide for the future. Going to your first question in terms of the EBITDA bridge and the contribution coming from this change in invoicing in Brazil, well, actually, the change in invoicing terms in Brazil was dilutive to the margin. This would come through in mix. Ironically, it included quite a lot of glyphosate, as we're exiting glyphosate, of course, we're still selling some, but the absolute impact on margin was below the group average, but they would come through the bridge in mix. Licensing yet again is within the bridge, but it's again going to come through -- coming through in bridge -- in the mix. Can I just say a few words about licensing, because I think it's important. Because I think now that we've got the VIPTERA registration now, we are expecting to see a significant increase in licensing income for our traits -- trait licensing income as we go forward. And we could see this expanding to up to just under about $500 million by 2020 and we'll start to ramp up in 2016, but that would come through largely in the mix line.

sections.speaker: Tony Jones
sections.content: Okay. So just a quick follow-up then, if that's okay. So if the gains to EBITDA were both mix from -- or mainly mix from South America and then licensing income, of that $437 million, could you give us a rough indication what proportion that represented?

sections.speaker: John Ramsay
sections.content: Overall, in the half, you can attribute probably about 50 basis points of margin improvement to mix overall.

sections.speaker: Jeremy Redenius
sections.content: It's Jeremy Redenius. I guess, firstly with the change in the timing of revenue recognition in Latin America, can you remind us of the -- how the timing works in the other regions please? And then secondly, when looking at your forward-looking EBITDA bridge to 2018, the price increases of 3%, can you talk about how you're thinking about implementing those please? I'm actually kind of wondering with the price increases, if that's -- that there's list price increasing, are we talking about product mix changes that you phased out like glyphosate, et cetera?

sections.speaker: John Ramsay
sections.content: Jeremy, so firstly on price, this is list pricing. This is increased revenue coming through to the bottom line and represents somewhere around about 1% price increase per annum, which is actually below what we've been able to achieve in the last few years, but that's a conservative assumption on pricing. The revenue recognition, let's just maybe spend a few moments on this in terms of why this has changed. We've previously been operating what we call the consumption model, which requires basically to get returns from all the distributors around the whole of Brazil in terms of the inventories that were held at those distribution locations. So that's the model we have been operating. And we've put that in place some years ago to give us absolute control over the levels of distributor inventories. For many of you who have been studying Brazil in this industry for some years, you will know that this has always been a problem in Brazil with the distributors overcommitting themselves and building up far too high levels of inventory, and this is our method of control, that hasn't changed. That's the fundamental point, that has not changed. We still get those returns from the distributors. We reward the distributors on the basis of their true sales of consumption into the market. We reward our sales force on that basis. The problem was -- is because of the sheer magnitude and the sheer complexity and the high volume of product moving at the December year-end, it was a becoming a problem for our revenue cutoff opposite our audit of the accounts. So last year, as the Audit Committee in February of 2015, we agreed with the Audit Committee that we'd look at ways of getting a better handle on this. And we basically negotiated with distributors, cut the ability of them to make returns and then move to a recording sales on invoicing and that's what we basically do in virtually every other market in the world. The important point about it is we have not changed the consumption model, which is an extremely good model for being able to control in-market inventories and being able to properly reward distributors and our sales force on true consumption.

sections.speaker: Sophie Jourdier
sections.content: I've got 3 questions as well, please. First of all, just on seed costs, which I think you mentioned was the reason for the rising cost of goods sold in the first half. Could you just explain that? I thought we were expecting Seed cost generally to reduce following the higher cost resource sort of a couple of years ago? That's the first question. The second question, I just wondered whether you could just explain in a little bit more detail the slide on -- the diagram on Slide 31 just talking about the corn traits revenue and royalty income sales increase towards about that $1 billion level. Could you just explain what exactly the different bars are and how that relates to the $500 million trade income you just talked about? I thought we were about $170-odd million at the moment. So just a bit more detail around what that is actually showing us at that chart. And the third question, and I'm sorry for bringing up the Monsanto takeover offer, but I appreciate that you've stated that the valuation they've offered is too low and the antitrust issues, but I wonder whether you could just explain why you haven't been prepared to do basic due diligence just given Monsanto's comments that they could be willing to raise the offer price under that scenario.

sections.speaker: Michael Mack
sections.content: Good morning, Sophie, it's Mike. I'll just take the Monsanto offer -- the Monsanto question quickly. I mean, look if you're -- if we're not proceeding with this proposal further, I don't see why there's -- why we don't -- the board don't see why there's any basis for conducting any due diligence and we were very robust with our answer back at the end of April when we first made it and then again in June. So no due diligence, no to the deal certainty, no to the price, no to the execution risk. It's all been said in over 3 months and nothing's really changed. John, on the corn trait revenue and the seed cost.

sections.speaker: John Ramsay
sections.content: Yes. But just, Sophie, on the seed costs, on the chart that you referred to, it's a $20 million benefit, so it is actually lower seed costs. Our color coding is whenever you see green is it's a good point. When you see gray, it's usually bad.

sections.speaker: Sophie Jourdier
sections.content: But yes -- but I thought you've mentioned that the oil price -- in your comment, that the oil price was the benefit and that there were some -- maybe I misheard that?

sections.speaker: John Ramsay
sections.content: No, sorry, sorry. No, the -- what we’re saying is the -- of the $20 million, the majority of it is seed costs. And there is -- you're right to say, I did say there's a small amount coming from oil, but the major contribution from oil this year, which is going to be about $15 million total for the year will be in the second half.

sections.speaker: Sophie Jourdier
sections.content: Okay. And then just on the corn traits diagram?

sections.speaker: John Ramsay
sections.content: Yes. On Slide 31, I think -- if you've got it in front of you, I just like to explain it a little bit better. The fundamental point here is that we are now about to see a ramp-up in trait royalty income largely, not exclusively, but largely associated with the registration we now have in VIPTERA, which enables licensees to proceed. Now on this chart, you've got an orange or yellow component. That is the amount of revenue that effectively is in Syngenta branded seed. Those are price paid for the trait when the grower buys the seeds, and that's the amount of revenue that's in that. We haven't shown that in the past as a separate amount, but it's put here just for completeness. The -- perhaps the more important thing is the 2 shades of gray, which is licensing income either through our licensing arm or through direct licensing to other seed companies, and that's the light gray and the dark gray. And as you can see in 2015, you're right to point out that that's a relatively small -- and I think the number in 2015 still is around $150 million, those 2 gray bars. Your reference to about $170 million is of total royalty income. This chart is just referring to trait royalty income. So the point here is that you see the expansion aided through our revenue generated from our own brands, but also you see the expansion in the revenue that's going to come from licensing income and is referring just to trait income.

sections.speaker: John Klein
sections.content: I've got 4 short questions. Firstly, on -- you have a slide on portfolio -- potential portfolio management of strategic options in Seeds, where you're talking about divestment or JV options. Can you maybe give us a bit more of an idea what the JV options would also include in North American Seeds business? Secondly, also on Seeds, the sugarcane business isn't mentioned in your presentation. Maybe you can give us an update on where we are with that? Thirdly, on soybean seeds, how much of the margin uplift that you're expecting in soybean gross margin is actually related to INTACTA sales? And then fourthly, on corn traits, the 2016 expectations for licensing income, how much of that is upfront payments or milestone payments versus acreage increase?

sections.speaker: Michael Mack
sections.content: John, maybe I can start first with the portfolio, the divestments and whether that would or would not include, I think you said, North American corn and soybean. First of all, I don't have -- I'm not going to go into a place where we're speculating about that. As John said, the tools and the various options for improving the profitability profile of these businesses, all of that is open to us. What you are hearing us say, though, very clearly, is trait income and the productivity of our trait pipeline is coming through and you can see that reflected in the margin of the underlying business in North America. And as we've been saying for years, these traits are portable. They're -- it's a source of revenue and income growth in Latin America. We're among the first now to market in Vietnam and the opportunity for this -- the corn seed and the traits continues to be good. But -- so I wouldn't say anything more about that. You asked about sugarcane. Yes, it's very much still in the portfolio and on offer. I don't -- for those of you who joined us in Brazil now over a year ago, you can see -- you will recall that we've made some investments in the CEEDS technology, C-E-E-D-S, and that's coming along nicely. The sugar market, of course, characterized by very low prices and it continues to be a difficult environment there. Syngenta is leading in Crop Protection. We've got great relationships with those sugarcane or ethanol producers, rather. And that continues to be an area of investment for us. John, there were 2 questions about the pull-through of INTACTA revenue on the soybean seeds in LATAM.

sections.speaker: John Ramsay
sections.content: Yes. I don't have a number, John, in my head for the INTACTA component, but the larger components are going to be in terms of revision to the business model, but there would be a few percentage points of margin that would be attributable to INTACTA in terms of the assumptions we're making about the market pricing. So far the question on trait revenue is concerned, it is -- going back to the slide that Sophie referred to on Slide 31, just to be clear, this is a cash basis so it does actually include some residual cash and receipts from DuPont in respect to the accounting that we recognized in 2012. But apart from that, it's all largely on an acreage volume basis, and a very, very large proportion of it is basically already within written agreements. So the largest part is going to be associated with third-party licensee volume increases.

sections.speaker: John Klein
sections.content: Okay. For the 2016 step-up that we're seeing on direct licenses, would that would be mostly attributable to acreage increases?

sections.speaker: John Ramsay
sections.content: For 2016, did you say?

sections.speaker: John Klein
sections.content: Yes.

sections.speaker: John Ramsay
sections.content: I believe so. I believe that's right, yes.

sections.speaker: Virginie Boucher-Ferte
sections.content: I've got, yes, 2 small questions, mainly clarification. You have a $6 billion to $6.5 billion of EBITDA target post 2020. Is it fair to assume that the realistic time frame to hit this target would be closer to 2025, 2030, assuming a 4% sales CAGR? If I'm reading your Slide 41 correctly, you've got a 4% growth assumption. My second question is on SOLATENOL. Do you have visibility -- more visibility on the timing of the SOLATENOL launch in Europe? Should we have it in our numbers for 2016?

sections.speaker: Michael Mack
sections.content: Good morning, Virginie. First on SOLATENOL, we continue to -- it's always a bit tricky to guess the regulators and I wouldn't want to overpromise and under-deliver that, but we continue to be optimistic that we may be in a position to have a registration in Q1 of next year. And if we have that registration, of course, that would be a 2016 revenue opportunity for us. So again, I don't want to get too far on the hook for that, but that is what we're working toward. And your question about the sales CAGR, I mean, John, you got that as the underlying assumption for the EBITDA evolution.

sections.speaker: John Ramsay
sections.content: Yes, yes. Thanks, Virginie. The slide you're referring to is the one on the bridge on Slide 41 and this is -- this was put together specifically to try and put a bit more -- now that we're well under way with our AOL delivery, that we thought we'd put this together to give a bit more indication of the components to get to the 24% to 26%. What we've adopted for the AOL program is a 4% sales growth and it's incorporated into this chart. So let me be clear, we are seeing that as a conservative assumption. So we put this together specifically on the 24% to 26% basis to try to provide a little bit more comfort around the headroom that we have. We still see our longer-term planning and it's -- our longer-term planning is based upon higher growth than 4%. But given the fact that we have -- we're in a market down period and the immediacy of trying to get the traction around our AOL program, we are basically setting our internal targets and passing those out to all parts of the business on the basis of not relying on volume growth above 4%. And -- but just to reinforce the point, from a long-term planning basis, we are still working at a higher level.

sections.speaker: Virginie Boucher-Ferte
sections.content: Okay. And sorry, just to be clear, when you mentioned that if the strategy in Seeds does not deliver, you would explore JV option and/or divestments, what time frame do you have in mind? Is it 2018 or could it be much sooner?

sections.speaker: John Ramsay
sections.content: It very much depends on the levels of confidence we can see in our plans. I think we're putting the plans into place as you know. If we start to see doubt either in terms of the ability to access the market, ability to get the profitability up, we'll start to consider how in fact we realize the strategic value inherent in these crops. So it very much depends on us and having the confidence in our momentum case in terms of delivery.

sections.speaker: Markus Mayer
sections.content: I have only question -- one question that is too many. On your net capital per sales, which was 1% higher year-over-year, could you give us some insight why this was the case?

sections.speaker: Michael Mack
sections.content: Markus, we couldn't hear you. The question was -- you said sales?

sections.speaker: Markus Mayer
sections.content: Yes. On your net on capital to sales ratio, which was 1 percentage points higher year-over-year, if you could give us some insights why this was the case?

sections.speaker: Michael Mack
sections.content: Yes, Markus, yes, that's clear. We've got -- essentially we've got different movements in inventory and in receivables and we are continuing to see efficiency in inventory. As you may recall last year, we had a very big push on inventory, made significant advance with a significant reduction in percentage of sales, and that is continuing at a lower level now in terms of the opportunity, but that's improving in inventory. We're finding receivables are going out, DSOs are going out a bit largely in Latin America, and I've spoken about this in the past that the -- in Latin America, we are seeing a considerable reduction in liquidity. Credit availability for growers is now seriously constrained. So we are having to watch that very carefully. We're putting a lot of effort into different financial solutions around barter and securitization and different working capital models with different banks. But the reality is that growers in Latin America are finding it very difficult to get access to credit.

sections.speaker: Markus Mayer
sections.content: And then an add-on question, does -- do you expect that this net on capital to sales ratio comes back to the levels you saw in 2014 then maybe in the second half of this year?

sections.speaker: John Ramsay
sections.content: I'd expect us to be overall a profile not dissimilar to the half year from the full year. The inventory will continue to show a modest improvement and receivables will continue to show a modest deterioration.

sections.speaker: Tony Jones
sections.content: I just had a question which related to the EBITDA bridge, Slide 41 this time. So just to clarify something, on this slide, you mentioned leverage twice, so there's a box with 2 percentage point gain from volume leverage, but then under the AOL box, you've got leverage, too, accounting for 40% of the 5% expansion. Is that leverage in that box also related to volume leverage or is it something else?

sections.speaker: John Ramsay
sections.content: Yes. Not very useful using the same word perhaps, Tony, sorry for that confusion. But volume leverage is just basically what is going to come through the 4% growth in the top line. The AOL savings are the incremental leverage we'll get by having the resources fixed opposite continuing growth. So basically, what it means is that specifically within the AOL program, you will have teams doing the same amount, the same level of operating for -- they'll manage the increase in activity associated with the same level of resource. So different ways in which they were able to manage the -- so instead of putting more resource in, they basically hold the resources constant whilst the business is growing and we're identifying that as over and above the level of leverage that would occur associated just with the ongoing volume growth. Very specifically targeted within the program, but we count that as part of the benefits attributable to those teams.

sections.speaker: Tony Jones
sections.content: Okay. Just sort of following up on the growth assumption then, so the 4%, is that all volume growth or is there anything in there from price, which is also in the bridge?

sections.speaker: John Ramsay
sections.content: That's the total growth. Of that 4%, 1% is price coming through in the 3-percentage-point improvement and 3% is volume coming through in the 2-percentage-point improvement.

sections.speaker: Michael Mack
sections.content: That concludes our presentation today, ladies and gentlemen. And I know if you have further questions, you'll contact Jennifer Gough and Lars Oestergaard in our Investor Relations and I look forward to seeing a number of you at our R&D Day the third week of September. Thank you for joining the call.
